author: @michaelward402
text: Abrasive polishing is not new by any means. Racing engine manufacturers do this on all moving parts, some parts to a lesser or greater degree depending on the part. Even to the extent of using a diamond based compound on highly stressed parts. IMHO I am however surprised that it would work well on plastic parts and would imagine Silicon lubes would have a big roll in reducing friction. It would of course depend on how good the original part was.

author: @rayadagio
text: The beauty of self-printed gears made of PLA is that they sort of self-polish and therefore improve over time on their own. Accelerating this process through polishing is an excellent idea. T-Cut is not familiar to me here in Germany. Does it have any special properties compared to other polishing pastes?

replies.author: @mark.guitar
replies.text: Nothing special. It's just cheap (and I already have a tube for buffing up guitar finishes in my day job!).

replies.author: @cyclic2696
replies.text: T-Cut is like abrasive Brasso (hoping you have Brasso in Germany!)  I use T-cut on the polyester piano case to polish out (some) scratches..

author: @prozacgod
text:
One extra note, it seems in a lot of these 3d prints I've been noticing in the last few years people adding gears to a lot of things, but they forget one thing.. gear ratios should not be rational.  If you make them irrational it means that every tooth in one gear is likely visited by every tooth on the other gear.

Instead of a 12 to 6 ratio  gearing maybe a 13:6 would work just fine?

I've been seeing this a lot, and figured it'd be a great reminder to toss out there.  There's a lot of people new to the whole idea of making gear trains and they don't know that concept

replies.author: @mark.guitar
replies.text: I have been using it on worm gears mainly, but that is a good point!

author: @kadmow
text: yep - many machine parts are lapped to very high quality finish - all potential metal on metal dynamic components should have a high finish specified - maybe it isn't common to be polishing the part mid shaft - but there are increments to be made (equal weight hollow shafts also last longer in high cyclle life applications (re. equal diameter as there is the tension and shear resistance to think of - that requires a stronger material)... Stress risers leading to failure are most common at the end of threads, splines and shoulders - a bit more of a buff at the transition may go a long way.

author: @lagunafishing
text: I was using TCut 30 years ago to smooth out blemishes on my polyurethane casts long before 3D printing became popular. These days I use TCut exclusively as a cutting paste to remove artefacts from 3D prints ready for moulding reproduction.

author: @philip5940
text:
So it's two concepts.    . Microcracks caused by torsional stress on an axle which can proceed to grow over time .The other is the lapping in of beaeing surfaces to minimise friction/resistance to movement .  In the popular mechanics magazine of 1950s , we see full page advertisements for something called superpolish . I never took the time to delve into what it was all about , it wasn't so easy like today .
  There's a kinda urban  legend story i heard long time ago about a Rolls Royce that broke an axle in the Swiss Alps. The irate recent new owner contacted Rolls Royce headquarters on a priority 24/7 number in the small hours . Within 1½ hours a helicopter with replacement axle and mechanics arrived to do a roadside replacement. They preferred to carry out the repairs in the wee dark hours when witnesses would be nowhere in sight. When he contacted the firm later during business hours to enquire about the cost , the representative told him he was perhaps mistaken and acted mystified about the helicopter episode "A Rolls Royce never breaks an axle " .

author: @thedevilinthecircuit
text: This same concept is used when building engines--especially when seating the valves.  A small amount of abrasive lapping compound is applied to the valve seat and the valve is inserted.  A little pressure is applied to the valve head while it's spun against the valve seat.  Done properly, it makes for a perfect contact seal.

author: @lorenbush8876
text: That was an interesting. I was going to say something for a while about the way your spinning gears sounding noisy, I had that problem with a hand crank generator I bought from some company out of China real cheap that I had to assemble myself, I put coconut oil or some other lubricant on the gears and it stopped most of the noise and it made it run much smoother. Also it occurred to me that  jewelry models could be made if they are smooth enough with the 3D printing for doing lost wax casting to make jewelry or anything else you want to cast from metal. Thanks Robert

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: someone suggested tumble polishing with sand - i liked the basics of the idea but i was thinking maybe toothpaste - the powder not the tube or maybe cerium oxide - what do you think?

replies.author: @lorenbush8876
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I don't know on plastic , I always used cerium oxide to polish opal and most other types of stone and it works well for that , all you can do is try it, I don't thing sand, if you polish it with a muslin buff use red or black jewelers rouge and run it light and slow and be careful not to melt the plastic, when I was in school they used something they called crystal that they used to polish watch crystals with, I don't remember if it were for both glass and plastic or not.  If you use a Dremel like polishing tool I would not use a foot control, use one that you can set the speed with a potentiometer so the speed is more steady and constant.
  I never have heard of anyone tumble polishing plastic, or using toothpaste on plastic, if the PLA is a hard type plastic there is a really fine cleanser powder called Cameo(Aluminum and Stainless Steel Cleaner) that works to polish paint pretty nicely I don't remember if I have ever tried it on plastic, you might need something like little pieces of felt or leather to help with rubbing it against the plastic, I don't know since I never have tried it.

author: @jsmxwll
text: I've done this a couple times. I make things tight enough that they can just move with a decent amount of friction then I use 3m rubbing compound and run it until it feels reasonably free, still a bit tighter feeling than I want it to be in the end. Clean it, grease it, check it and repeat until I get the feel I want. I've gotten some very nice results this way.

author: @jamesross1003
text: That is a very ingenious partial solution.  I say partial because there is only so much that polishing something can do by itself.  After doing the T-Cut trick, why not disassemble what you are working with and then rub in graphite powder(the finer the better).  T-Cut polish to start and graphite to finish I would think on many materials(3d printed included) would certainly help to give it a bit more longevity and reduce wear on it.  Something else to ponder though is the fact that graphene bonds very well with other carbons.  Would it be possible to graphite coat and then graphene coat for a more smooth surface.  Strangely enough it's mulling over such questions that keep me awake and sleepless some nights.  LOL!  Cheers!  Thanks for the video Rob!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: like it mate - cheers

replies.author: @KaiserTom
replies.text: I figure you could just use graphene directly in that case. PLA or any other plastics are all hydrocarbons. So I would imagine graphene bonds to it by itself pretty nicely.

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @KaiserTom Probably.  I was just throwing around the idea that it might make it have less friction.  To add it to the material would make it harder in theory, but maybe not have less friction.  It would still help to add some kind of lubricant even after the addition of it to the material.

author: @davidblyth5495
text:
A very interesting issue. It's one of the considerations we learnt of during our undergraduate material science course.
Working with high frequency electrical structures the surface roughness has an influence on insertion loss.
So 3 D printing or sputtering technology has the drawback of rough surfaces.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very much so mate

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: Makes sense . The high frequency current travels along the surface rather than within the core of a conductor.

author: @William_Hada
text: Great idea! I wonder if one more step after T-cut  of solvent vapor polishing would make the plastic surface even smoother/stronger.. In the case of ABS plastic, one can hang the T-cut polished part in a chamber full of acetone vapor for a few minutes to vapor polish the surface even smoother. I use an empty paint can with a little acetone in the bottom. The can sides are also lined with paper towels held on by small magnets to help distribute the acetone vapor.

author: @donniewatson9120
text: Abrasive lapping is a great way to get two items to fit well. I've used the process numerous times when rebuilding engines to make sure the valves are properly seating into the valves seats their mated to. This minimizes the opportunity for them to leak by, which minimizes the risk of burnt valves.

author: @russellzauner
text: That's a smart move.  We used to use either vapor polishing or a coating/solvent (several are available for different materials) to chemically smooth parts but also it would help with delamination as it would fuse the outside of the layers together - sometimes when mechanical polishing was applied it could tear apart weakly built and or more delicate parts.  But this would actually grind them to very good tolerances - perhaps a smoothing/fusing step *then* the surface grinding with pastes (unless you're fusing from friction heat, which means you're deforming the part when working as well as a higher risk of delamination starting).  Fused and trued.  Nice.

author: @grendel1960a
text: on metal gears where a perfect fit is required the process uses a grinding paste and is called lapping, similarly the inlet and exhaust valves of cars used to be lapped to get the airtight seal required for combustion, chrome polish or toothpaste also work well with plastics (toothpaste can also be used to polish fogged headlight lenses.

replies.author: @RTR-MATEY
replies.text: @grendel1960a  Ahhh Sooo!!! Thanks!!! Makes Absolute Sense!!! Also helps if the extrusion factor is properly calculated too!!! If I'm printing 0.20mm layer height, I always set extrusion factor to 0.95 and if I'm doing a layer height of 0.16mm (excellent for overhangs!!!) I set the extrusion factor at 0.85. Additionally, I've disabled the gcode M413 power loss recovery as I have my 3D printer connected to an Uninterruptable Power Supply (UPS). This has eliminated all blobs at each point where the machine writes the recovery code to the SD Card and pauses, thus causing the blobs.
